Title: Serdang deputy police chief dies of heart attack at 51
Article:
SERDANG: Serdang deputy police chief Superintendent Shahnaz Akhtar Haji has died.
He is understood to have suffered a heart attack and died at Serdang Hospital about 10am.
Selangor police chief Datuk Shazeli Kahar confirmed the news.
"He passed away at the age of 51 and had served in the police force for 30 years," he said when contacted.
He extended his condolences to the family, describing the passing as a significant loss to the Selangor police contingent.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Police recover four urns, record 10 statements in Nilai urn theft case
Article:
NILAI: Police have recorded statements from 10 witnesses and recovered four urns containing human ashes as investigations continue into the theft that has shocked families and the community.
Nilai police chief Superintendent Johari Yahya said the probe was ongoing, with investigators working to complete witness statements and verify the identities of the ashes in the recovered urns.
"So far, 10 witnesses have had their statements recorded. Four urns have been found, but full verification and checks are still being carried out by the management," he said.
He said police are looking at all angles, including the possibility of insider involvement.
"However, this is still at an early stage and cannot be confirmed at this point," he added.
It was reported that more than 50 urns were reported stolen from three memorial parks.
They include:
* 22 urns from the Perpetual Memorial Park in Kulai, Johor on March 9.
* 30 urns from the Xiao En Memorial Park in Nilai, Negri Sembilan on March 18.
* The theft of an undisclosed amount of urns from the Nirvana Asia Group.
Reports have indicated that families have been contacted with ransom demands, although details are sketchy.
Nirvana had said the urns which were stolen from their columbarium were returned. However, there was no mention if a ransom was paid.
Johari also said police are analysing whether the modus operandi bears any similarity to the cases reported in Kulai.
"At this stage, we are conducting comparisons and analysis, but no links have been established," he said.
He added that investigators are also seeking technical assistance, including analysing communication and transaction elements linked to the case.
Johari said police would continue to provide updates as the investigation progresses.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Appeals Court trims jail term of man who murdered senior citizen
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: An Indian national sentenced to 38 years in prison by the Shah Alam High Court for fatally hammering a man at a senior citizens' home in 2020 has had his sentence reduced by three years by the Court of Appeal (CoA).
A three-judge panel led by Datuk Azman Abdullah upheld the High Court's conviction of Alagesan Kamaraj but allowed his appeal on sentence.
Alagesan was ordered to serve his term from his arrest on Sept 10, 2020, and receive 12 strokes of the rotan.
In the written judgment, Azman, along with judges Datuk Mohamed Zaini Mazlan and Datuk Azmi Ariffin, unanimously agreed that a 35-year jail term with 12 strokes of whipping was appropriate.
The judges had considered submissions from both the prosecution and defence; and applied their discretion provided under the Abolition of the Death Penalty Act and the amended Section 302 of the Penal Code for murder.
The judges noted that the High Court had taken into account the accused's early guilty plea, which saved judicial time, but was not inclined to grant a one-third reduction in sentence as the offence was heinous and warranted a deterrent punishment.
"After reading the written submissions filed, hearing both parties orally and perusing the records of appeal, we unanimously allowed the appeal on sentence.
"We applied our discretion provided under the Act which conferred on this court the discretion to impose either the sentence of death or sentence of imprisonment of not less than 30 years and not more than 40 years.
"We therefore impose a sentence of 35 years imprisonment with 12 strokes of whipping," he said.
Alagesan had pleaded guilty to murdering Sugan Ganesan about 3pm at Templer Senior Citizen's Care Home in Petaling Jaya on Sept 10, 2020.
The charge was read and explained to him in Tamil by an interpreter.
The High Court judge found him guilty, convicted him for murder and sentenced to 38 years in jail with 15 strokes of the rotan.
Alagesan initially filed an appeal against both the conviction and his sentence.
However, he withdrew the challenge on conviction during the CoA hearing on July 21 last year.
Azman said the High Court found that Alagesan had killed the deceased at the home.
"He came to the living room where the deceased was sleeping and took a hammer from under the table in the living room and struck the deceased's head with several blows.
"A witness named Ramasamy Subaramaniam, who was sleeping next to the deceased, woke up and saw the appellant (Alagesan) holding the hammer and leaving the scene," he said.
The injuries suffered by the deceased were consistent with a post mortem report which revealed that the cause of death was due to cranio cerebral injuries due to blunt trauma.
Alagesan was represented by defence lawyer Jayananda Rao while deputy public prosecutor Solehah Noratikah Ismail appeared for the Attorney-General's Chambers.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Lorry driver detained after 'reckless' driving chase in Kulim
Article:
KULIM: A 27-year-old lorry driver with a prior drug offence record has been arrested following a high-speed police chase in Padang Serai.
He had evaded arrest on Monday after driving recklessly during the pursuit.
He drove recklessly, crashed through a roadside tent and tested positive for methamphetamine.
Police, however, said the suspect was apprehended at about 8pm yesterday during a raid at Kampung Sidam Kanan.
The incident, captured on closed-circuit television and widely shared online, showed the vehicle speeding through a residential area with sirens before crashing through the tent.
Kulim deputy police chief Deputy Superintendent Tengku Mohd Faisal Tengku Yeng said the incident began at about 9.45pm (on Monday), when a patrol unit spotted a man in a blue Perodua Kancil behaving suspiciously near a supermarket in Taman Serai Wangi.
Police tailed the suspect and attempted to stop the vehicle using sirens and beacon lights, but the driver refused to comply and sped off towards Taman Lagenda.
"During the chase, the suspect drove recklessly through a residential area before crashing into a tent with tables and chairs set up outside a house, and continued fleeing towards Taman Mahsuri.
"He later collided with another vehicle at a junction and escaped towards Sungai Petani," he said in a statement today.
Police said the suspect tested positive for methamphetamine and was found in possession of 0.30g  of suspected syabu at the time of his arrest.
Authorities also seized the Perodua Kancil and the drugs. The suspect has one prior record related to drug offences.
He has been remanded for four days until March 28 to assist investigations.
The case is being investigated under Section 186 of the Penal Code for obstructing a public servant, Section 42(1) of the Road Transport Act 1987 for reckless and dangerous driving, as well the Dangerous Drugs Act 1952.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MMEA rescues five Thai crew after cargo vessel fire off Johor coast
Article:
KOTA TINGGI: Five Thai crew members were rescued after a cargo vessel caught fire in its galley off the Johor coast yesterday.
The men, aged between 25 and 61, sustained burns, with some injuries described as serious.
Acting Tanjung Sedili Maritime Zone director, Maritime Commander Mohd Najib Sam, said the incident occurred about 30 nautical miles east of Tanjung Sedili at around 10.20am.
The Johor Bahru Maritime Rescue Sub Centre (MRSC) received a distress call at 12.20pm and deployed the Pekasa 30 boat to carry out a medical evacuation (medevac) operation.
"The fire was successfully brought under control by the crew before assistance arrived," he said in a statement.
MMEA personnel conducted an initial assessment at the scene before transporting all victims ashore for further treatment.
The operation concluded after all five crew members were safely evacuated and handed over to medical personnel.
"We remain committed to ensuring the safety of the nation's waters and providing immediate assistance to the maritime community in need," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: DBKL fallen tree suit: Routine maintenance not a choice, but life-saving responsibility
Article:
The court's decision requiring Kuala Lumpur City Hall (DBKL) to pay compensation amounting to RM824,180 to the family of a young victim killed by a falling tree is both significant and telling.
It serves as a powerful reminder that local authorities have a duty of care to ensure that public infrastructure and facilities under their jurisdiction are properly maintained at all times.
This tragic incident, which was not the first, highlights a critical issue of maintenance.
While planting trees and beautifying cities are commendable initiatives, these efforts must be accompanied by consistent inspection, risk assessment, and timely maintenance.
Without proper care, roadside trees, especially ageing or weakened ones, can become serious hazards, particularly during storms, heavy rain, or strong winds.
This responsibility extends beyond trees.
It includes potholes, damaged walkways, faulty streetlights, unstable structures, and other public amenities.
Any lapse in maintenance can expose the public to unnecessary danger and lead to preventable loss of life or injury.
The judgment underscores that public safety cannot be compromised.
Local authorities must adopt a proactive approach by:
* Conducting regular tree health inspections and structural assessments
* Identifying high-risk areas, especially along busy roads and public spaces
* Implementing preventive pruning and removal of dangerous trees;
* Establishing rapid response teams during extreme weather conditions;
* Strengthening inspection and maintenance of roads and public facilities; and,
* Keeping proper records and accountability mechanisms
This ruling also sends a clear message that when negligence leads to harm, affected parties have the right to seek justice through the courts.
Public authorities entrusted with managing shared spaces must therefore exercise the highest level of diligence and responsibility.
Ultimately, prevention is always better than compensation. No amount of money can replace a life lost.
What is needed is a strong culture of maintenance, risk management, and safety-first thinking across all local authorities.
Let this decision serve as a wake-up call for all local councils nationwide to review their maintenance systems, strengthen enforcement, and prioritise public safety.
Public safety must never be left to chance — proper maintenance saves lives.
Tan Sri Lee Lam Thye
Chairman
Alliance For a Safe Community
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Penang man's RON95 purchase in viral video is legal, says KPDN
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: The Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Ministry (KPDN) confirmed that
no offence was committed in a viral incident
showing a man filling RON95 petrol into a plastic container at a petrol station in Bayan Baru.
Penang KPDN director S. Jegan said enforcement officers were despatched to the location following complaints and conducted further investigations into the matter.
"The man seen in the video is a fisherman with a valid permit to purchase fuel in accordance with existing laws, and subject to a stipulated quota.
"His fuel purchase complied with regulations and did not involve any wrongdoing," he said in a statement today.
Jegan said any purchase exceeding 20 litres into containers required a special controlled goods permit issued by the ministry.
He said such permits were granted for specific uses, including agricultural and fisheries operations, and subjected to strict conditions.
"KPDN conducts continuous monitoring at petrol stations to ensure compliance with regulations," he added.
Selling fuel into containers exceeding 20 litres without a valid permit is an offence under the Control of Supplies Act and may result in stern action.
A video went viral yesterday showing a man filling a plastic container with RON95 petrol at a station on Jalan Tengah, Bayan Baru.
The plastic container was placed in the boot of his car.
While some netizens called on the authorities to take action, others came to the man's defence.
The incident came hot on the heels of a similar incident of a couple filling multiple containers with RON95 petrol in the boot of a car at a station in Skudai.
KPDN Minister
Datuk Armizan Mohd Ali has warned
that the sale and filling of fuel into containers without a special permit is an offence.
LINK:
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: It's go time: Historic Moon mission set for lift-off
Article:
WASHINGTON: More than half-a-century after the groundbreaking Apollo programme's last crewed flight to the Moon, three men and one woman are preparing for a lunar journey set to turn a new page in American space exploration.
The long-delayed NASA mission dubbed Artemis II is slated to lift-off from Florida and venture to Earth's natural satellite as early as April 1.
They won't land but are instead on a mission to fly by, much as Apollo 8 did in 1968.
Americans Reid Wiseman, Victor Glober and Christina Koch – along with Canadian Jeremy Hansen – will carry out the approximately 10-day trip.
The odyssey will mark a series of firsts: the first time a woman, a person of colour and a non-American will venture on a Moon mission.
It's also the inaugural crewed flight of NASA's new lunar rocket, dubbed SLS.
The mammoth orange-and-white rocket is designed to allow the United States to repeatedly return to the Moon in years to come, with the goal of establishing a permanent base that will offer a stepping stone for further exploration.
"We're going back to the Moon because it's the next step in our journey to Mars," said Wiseman, the Artemis II commander, on a NASA podcast.
- Space Race 2.0? -
The Artemis programme – named in honour of Apollo's goddess twin – aims to test technologies needed to one day send humans to Mars, a far more distant journey.
That ambition presents an immense challenge – which is compounded by pressure to achieve it before China does.
China is currently aiming to land humans on the Moon by 2030.
Beijing is also targetting the lunar South Pole, not least for its rich natural resource potential.
The competition recalls the 1960s-era Space Race between the US and the Soviet Union – but Harvard professor Matthew Hersch said that rivalry was "unique" and "will not be repeated anytime soon."
He told AFP the Chinese are "not really competing with anyone but themselves."
Washington's lunar programme investment is significantly lower now than during the Cold War era – but the technology has changed dramatically.
"The computer technology that supports the Artemis 2 crew would be almost unimaginable to the Apollo 8 crew, which went to the Moon in a spacecraft with the electronics of a modern high-end toaster oven," Hersch said.
And yet the Artemis 2 mission will not be without risks, even by NASA's own admission.
The crew will board a spacecraft that has never once carried humans or traveled to the Moon, which is more than 384,000 kilometres (238,855 miles) from Earth – or roughly 1,000 times further away than the International Space Station.
"We don't accept anything less than perfect, otherwise we're accepting greater risk," NASA's former chief astronaut Peggy Whitson told AFP.
"That is an important process that everyone has to embrace in order for us to be really successful, because we have to live with that knowledge, because of our space flight history, that when accidents happen, people will die," she said.
Minimising risks and preventing disaster will involve the crew performing a series of checks and maneuvers while still in Earth's vicinity.
If all goes well, they'll set forth for the Moon.
- Ambitious timeline -
The crew's objective will be to verify that both the rocket and the spacecraft are in working order, in the hopes of paving the way for a return and Moon landing in 2028 – the final year of President Donald Trump's term.
That deadline has raised eyebrows among experts, in part because Washington is relying on the private sector's technological headway.
The astronauts will require a second vehicle to descend to the moon's surface, a lunar lander that remains under development by rival space companies owned by billionaires Elon Musk and Jeff Bezos.
The Artemis programme has also been plagued by delays and massive cost overruns.
Still, NASA hopes that Artemis 2 can succeed in recreating the rare moment of unity and hope that Apollo 8, when a crew flew by the Moon on Christmas Eve 1968, allowed for.
In the shadow of a tumultuous year, approximately one billion people worldwide tuned in to their flickering television sets to follow the monumental journey of Frank Borman, Jim Lovell and Bill Anders.
The astronauts – who immortalised the famous "Earthrise" photograph taken from lunar orbit – were credited with having "saved 1968."
Nearly 60 years later, the country is once again mired in deep division and uncertainty, and the crew of Artemis 2 will soon have their chance to inspire. - AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Teen dies after fall from seventh floor of apartment building
Article:
SHAH ALAM: A teenage boy died yesterday after reportedly falling from the seventh floor of an apartment building in Section 19, Shah Alam.
Shah Alam police chief Assistant Commissioner Ramsay Embol said police received a report at 6.44pm yesterday.
"Police initially suspected the victim had fallen from the fifth floor, but further investigations indicate he likely fell from the seventh floor of the building."
"He was found unconscious by members of the public on the ground floor at 4.20pm.
"He was rushed to Shah Alam Hospital for treatment but was pronounced dead at 5.40pm," he said in a statement today.
Investigations at the scene found no elements of crime and the case was classified as sudden death.
A post-mortem will be conducted today at Shah Alam Hospital to determine the cause of death.
Those with information can contact 011-39360779 or the Shah Alam police headquarters at 03-55202222 to assist in investigations.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Fire at Kuwait airport after drones hit fuel tank
Article:
KUWAIT CITY: Drones hit a fuel tank and sparked a fire at Kuwait International Airport, the Gulf state's civil aviation authority said on Wednesday, as Iran presses on with its attacks in the nearly four-week regional war.
Citing preliminary information, the Directorate General of Civil Aviation said in a statement posted online that the attack had caused only "limited" damage and no casualties.
Firefighters were working to bring the blaze under control, said agency spokesman Abdullah Al-Rajhi.
The Kuwait National Guard meanwhile said in a statement that its forces intercepted six drones early Wednesday, and the army said air defences were "responding to hostile missile and drone attacks."
Elsewhere in the Gulf, Saudi Arabia said it had intercepted four drones in the kingdom's east.
The Kuwait airport is largely closed to commercial flights and has come under attack several times since the regional war began on February 28, when Israel and the United States launched strikes on Iran.
On March 14, the civil aviation authority said an attack with "several drones" targeted the airport and "struck its radar system." No casualties were reported.
Drones hit fuel tanks at the airport on March 8, and an earlier attack on a passenger terminal left several people mildly wounded and caused some damage.
Major airlines have suspended flights to the Gulf, or cut back due to fuel shortages linked to the war. - AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump sends peace plan as Iran opens to 'non-hostile' oil vessels
Article:
TEHRAN: US President Donald Trump sent a peace plan to Iran as he voiced optimism Tuesday at ending nearly a month of warfare, with Tehran announcing that it will let "non-hostile" oil vessels go through the crucial Strait of Hormuz.
The tentative signs of a diplomatic solution came despite new violence, with an Iranian missile causing injuries in Israel, which in turn pressed on multiple fronts and vowed to seize control of a strip of southern Lebanon.
Trump, whose pronouncements in recent days have swung wildly from vowing massive attacks on Iran to declaring the nearly month-long war virtually over, said the United States was "in negotiations right now" with Iran – which has not confirmed any formal talks.
"They did something yesterday that was amazing actually. They gave us a present and the present arrived today. And it was a very big present worth a tremendous amount of money," Trump told reporters in the Oval Office.
"That meant one thing to me – we're dealing with the right people."
He did not explain further but said it related to the Strait of Hormuz, which Iran has largely blockaded in retaliation for US and Israeli strikes, sending global energy prices soaring.
Iran, in a message circulated by the International Maritime Organization shortly afterward, assured safe passage to "non-hostile vessels" going through the strait, the gateway for one-fifth of the world's oil.
Iran had already in recent days said it was not targeting friendly nations, although many vessels have shied away as insurance companies refuse to take risks.
The benchmark price of crude oil dropped close to six percent after the latest developments. Prices at the pump have soared in the United States after the war by Trump, causing him a political headache.
- New nuclear deal? -
Trump had earlier threatened to "obliterate" Iran's power plants, which some argue would be a war crime, if it did not open the strait by late Monday Washington time. Before US markets opened Monday, Trump abruptly extended that deadline by five days, citing diplomatic progress.
Pakistan's prime minister has offered to host US-Iran talks, which Trump said involved top officials including Vice President JD Vance.
Trump said that he had sent a plan and that it "all starts with, they cannot have a nuclear weapon."
The New York Times, quoting unnamed officials, said that the United States had sent the 15-point plan to Iran through Pakistan.
Israel's Channel 12 said that Trump was proposing a one-month ceasefire during which the sides would discuss a proposal that would include handing over Iran's enriched uranium and banning further enrichment.
Iran would also ensure safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz.
Iran in turn would see an end to all sanctions, which have been in place in various forms for years, the Israeli report said.
Iran would also receive assistance in developing civil nuclear energy at Bushehr, a key site which dates from before the 1979 Islamic revolution.
Iran on Tuesday accused Israel of carrying out a second strike on Bushehr, which lies perilously close to Gulf Arab population centers.
"The sounds, the explosions, the missiles – they are part of our daily life now," a 35-year-old woman in Tehran told AFP by telephone. "Our one real worry now is that our oil and gas infrastructure isn't targeted by missile strikes."
Iran had agreed in 2015 to broad restraints on its contested nuclear program in a deal that Trump ripped up during his first term as he joined Israel in applying pressure to the cleric-run state.
The reported new proposal would keep in place the Islamic republic which weeks earlier ruthlessly crushed mass protests, killing thousands, despite earlier vows of regime change by Trump and especially Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu.
- Troops en route despite diplomacy -
Despite Trump's stated hopes for diplomacy, The Wall Street Journal reported that the United States is planning to send 3,000 soldiers from the elite 82nd Airborne Division to the Middle East.
Trump's envoys were negotiating a nuclear deal with Iran just two days before the United States and Israel launched the massive attack on February 28, killing Iran's supreme leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei on the first day.
Iranian missiles have found growing success penetrating Israeli defences, with AFP images showing rubble-strewn streets in the commercial hub Tel Aviv. On Tuesday, more than a dozen people were injured in Israel, including an infant, first responders said.
Israel said it conducted a "large wave" of airstrikes across several areas of Iran. Israeli military spokesman Effie Defrin said his country's war plan was "unchanged" despite Trump's remarks and that it would continue "to deepen the damage and remove existential threats."
Israel has also stepped up its campaign against the Iran-backed Hezbollah militant group in Lebanon, saying its military would take control of south Lebanon up to the Litani river, around 30 kilometres (20 miles) from the border.
Israel – which occupied southern Lebanon for nearly two decades until 2000 – carried out new strikes across the country. The Israeli military late Tuesday warned residents of Beirut's southern suburbs, strongholds of Hezbollah, to evacuate in the face of imminent strikes.
The Israeli campaign has killed at least 1,072 people in Lebanon, with more than one million people displaced, according to authorities. Another nine people died in Israeli strikes in the south, officials said.
Lebanon was pulled into the Middle East war when Hizbullah began firing rockets into Israel on March 2 to avenge the killing of Khamenei.
Lebanon, whose central government has long been fragile, grew increasingly assertive by announcing it was ordering the Iranian ambassador to leave by Sunday, accusing the Islamic republic of meddling and commanding Hezbollah operations.
Bahrain, Kuwait, the United Arab Emirates and Saudi Arabia all said they had intercepted renewed drone and missile attacks as Iran kept up retaliatory strikes on US-allied Gulf states.
Kuwait reported a fire at its main airport after drones hit a fuel tank. - AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Dialog, Velesto gain momentum
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Dialog Bhd and Velesto Energy Bhd are drawing renewed investor interest as both counters flash bullish technical signals, pointing to a potential extension of their near-term uptrends, RHB Research said.
Dialog, in particular, is showing signs of extending its upward momentum after staging a convincing bullish breakout, signalling a shift in near-term market sentiment.
The research house noted that Dialog formed a long bullish candlestick and closed decisively above the RM2.05 resistance level, a technical development that points to strengthening buying interest and accelerating upward momentum. The breakout is seen as a key inflection point, suggesting that the counter may be entering a new phase of price appreciation after a period of consolidation.
RHB Research said the move above resistance reflects improving investor confidence and could pave the way for further gains in the sessions ahead.
"After the breakout, it may test the next resistance at RM2.20, followed by the RM2.35 level. In the event the stock falls below the RM1.90 support, this will negate the bullish setup," the firm said in a note.
Year-to-date (YTD), the stock has risen 17.1 per cent, or 31 sen, bringing the company's market capitalisation to RM11.7 billion.
RHB Research said that Velesto Energy is also gaining traction and appears poised to sustain its bullish trajectory after breaking above a critical resistance level.
The stock registered a strong bullish candlestick, successfully crossing above the 21-day simple moving average (SMA) and closing above the 33 sen mark. This combination of signals indicates that short-term momentum is strengthening, supported by improving sentiment towards the counter.
The breakout reinforces the view that Velesto Energy's near-term trend remains positive, with momentum likely to carry the stock higher.
On the back of this development, the stock could advance towards the 35 sen level, with the next upside target at 37 sen, it added.
Nevertheless, the research house cautioned that downside risks remain. A pullback below the 31.5 sen support level would invalidate the bullish outlook and point to a potential weakening in momentum.
YTD, the stock had gained 17.86 per cent, giving the company a market cap of RM2.72 billion.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Woman wails during Tangkak roadside confrontation [WATCH]
Article:
TANGKAK: A heated roadside confrontation erupted in Tangkak, Johor, as two men shouted at each other while bystanders restrained them and a woman wailed continuously.
The cause of the dispute and the relationships between those involved could not be verified.
Police are aware of the incident, an official told the New Straits Times.
The altercation unfolded in front of a popular sports outlet, where about a dozen men and women tried to prevent an apparent physical fight between two men.
At one point, a man is heard shouting, "this is my wife," while another challenges him, saying, "let's go one on one."
A woman in a blue baju kurung appears visibly distressed, while another woman in a pink shirt is seen holding her hand.
In a separate clip, the woman in blue is seen crying as she gathers her belongings from the vehicle.
Bystanders urge the two men — one in a white shirt and the other seated in a car — to settle the matter at a nearby police station. Voices can be heard saying: "Settle it at the police station."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Boy, 7, killed in Sungai Petani road accident
Article:
SUNGAI PETANI: A seven-year-old boy was killed after being hit by a car while crossing a road at Jalan Kota Kuala Muda here.
Kuala Muda district police chief Assistant Commissioner Hanyan Ramlan said police received information at 9.45pm on an accident involving a car that struck a pedestrian at Kota Kuala Muda.
He said initial investigations found the incident is believed to have occurred at 9.23pm.
"The driver was travelling from Kota Kuala Muda towards Pulau Sayak when the child suddenly crossed from the left into the path of the vehicle.
"The driver was unable to avoid the collision due to the close distance and struck the child.
"As a result of the impact, the victim sustained injuries and was taken to Kota Kuala Muda Health Clinic, where he was pronounced dead," he said.
Hanyan added that the victim's body was sent to the Forensic Unit at Hospital Sultan Abdul Halim (HSAH) for further action.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: CapitaLand Ascendas REIT to buy industrial, data centre assets for US$1.1bil
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Singapore's CapitaLand Ascendas REIT said on Tuesday it will but interests in three assets in Singapore and Japan for S$1.41 billion (US$1.1 billion), including fees and expenses, as it aims to bolster its income base and diversify its portfolio.
Here are some details:
* The acquisitions include a 100 per cent stake in 25 Loyang Crescent, a logistics and industrial asset in Singapore, for S$504.2 million, and a 50 per cent stake in Ascent, a business space property, for S$245 million.
* A global sovereign wealth fund will own the remaining 50 per cent of Ascent.
* The real estate investment trust will also enter Japan through the purchase of a 49 per cent stake in a Tier III hyperscale data centre in Greater Osaka for S$620.7 million.
* A fund managed by a unit of Japanese conglomerate Mitsui & Co will hold the remaining 51 per cent.
* Following the transactions, the REIT's Singapore assets under management will rise to about S$13.2 billion, representing roughly 66 per cent of its enlarged portfolio value of S$19.9 billion.
* The Osaka data centre marks the REIT's first investment in Japan and is aimed at capturing growth in one of Asia-Pacific's largest and fastest-growing data centre markets, driven by demand for artificial intelligence and cloud-computing infra.
* Separately, CapitaLand Ascendas REIT proposed to launch an equity fund raising to secure gross proceeds of at least around S$900 million, which will be used to partially finance acquisitions, including the assets in Singapore and Japan.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Europe hit by prime office supply crunch as construction slumps
Article:
LONDON: Construction of new office space in Europe has slumped to its lowest in a decade, even as rents in prime locations hit record highs, research shows, deepening a supply crunch that is forcing many occupants to stay put.
The conflict in the Middle East poses further risk to the sector's outlook as rising energy prices could add to inflationary pressure, according to a report by property agency Cushman & Wakefield.
After emptying out during the Covid-19 pandemic, offices from London to Paris are filling up again as companies compel staff to come in on more days. That has helped underpin 20 straight quarters of rental growth for prime offices in Europe, according to the research.
However, space under construction fell to 10.1 million square feet at the end of last year, the lowest level since 2016, the report said, citing the impact of high construction and financing costs.
Demand for new office space in London is at over 11 million square feet, about 20 per cent above the long-term average, according to separate research from agency Knight Frank, which also predicted a looming supply crunch.
Nearly a third of occupiers will end up staying put due to a lack of choice or high prices, it added.
"You can't turn the tap on overnight for supply," Brad Hyler, co-president of Brookfield's real estate group, told Reuters at the Canadian investor's recently completed central London tower, the 35-storey One Leadenhall.
The Middle East conflict could weigh on property deals and financing in the short term, Hyler said, but added this was too early to predict and Brookfield still expected a gradual recovery.
RECORD RENTS
Developers like Brookfield that have delivered new office towers post-pandemic have benefited from a dearth of competition.
One Leadenhall's anchor tenant, US law firm Latham & Watkins, recently decided to take up extra space in the building, including the top floor at 160 pounds (US$214) per square foot - thought to be a record for the City of London financial district - a source familiar with the matter said. Brookfield declined to comment. The tower - which rises above the City's historic Leadenhall market - is now fully let. Investment in European office construction totalled 52 billion euros (US$60 billion) in 2025, up 14 per cent on the prior year but still roughly half the 10-year average, according to Cushman & Wakefield.
A flight to quality office buildings by occupiers meant a record 52 per cent of all space leased across Europe, the Middle East and Africa last year was the highest quality, the research showed.
The vacancy rate for this premium space fell to 3.5 per cent at the end of last year, while the overall vacancy rate remained steady at 9.8 per cent.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Firecracker shop owner detained after blaze in Kota Baru
Article:
KOTA BARU: The owner of a firecracker premises that caught fire in Wakaf Che Yeh yesterday has been detained for further investigation.
The 44-year-old woman, who also owns the eatery, was detained along with her 38-year-old employee at 8.30pm last night.
Kota Baru district police chief Assistant Commissioner Mohd Azmir Damiri said checks found the woman had a permit to sell firecrackers and fireworks at the location of the incident.
Fire and Rescue Department forensics identified the cause of the blaze as a short circuit from an electrical wire connection linking the eatery to the coloured lights of the firecracker sales tent.
"At 8.30pm last night, police arrested two women, including the premises owner, for investigation under Section 285 of the Penal Code," he said, according to Harian Metro.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Empire Sushi chain owner aims for RM254mil in Bursa debut at 70 sen IPO
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Empire Premium Food Bhd, the operator of the Empire Sushi chain, is set to raise up to RM254.1 million with its upcoming debut on the Main Market of Bursa Malaysia next month.
The company has set its initial public offering (IPO) price at 70 sen per share, according to the prospectus launched on Wednesday. The listing will raise RM152.6 million for the company and RM96.3 million for Jordan Tan and Nicole Lim, the husband-and-wife co-founders of Empire Premium.
The prospectus states that RM79.1 million, or 51.8 per cent of the proceeds, will be used to expand 56 new outlets across Malaysia, while RM12.6 million, or 8.3 per cent, will go towards refurbishing existing outlets. A further RM52.1 million, or 34.1 per cent, will strengthen working capital, and RM8.8 million is earmarked for IPO-related expenses.
The IPO comprises 218 million issue shares, alongside 145 million existing shares offered for sale, bringing the total offering to 363 million shares.
A total of 293 million shares, including 145 million offer shares and 148 million issue shares, will be allocated for institutional investors, while 137.5 million shares are reserved for Bumiputera investors under approval from MITI.
The remaining 70 million shares comprise 55 million shares open to the Malaysian public, of which 27.5 million are reserved for Bumiputera investors and 15 million shares are set aside for applications by eligible company personnel.
Upon full subscription, the company would be valued at RM254.1 million, while its current total market capitalisation stands at RM770 million.
Applications for the retail tranche will close on March 31, with order-taking for the institutional tranche ending on April 1. The final IPO price will be set at the lower of the retail or institutional book-building price.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Quadruple amputee charged with murder after shooting passenger in car
Article:
MARYLAND: An American quadruple amputee is facing a murder charge after he allegedly shot and killed a man in a car during an argument.
Dayton Webber, 27, a professional American Cornhole League athlete, is accused of killing Bradrick Wells, 27, following a dispute on Sunday night in La Plata, Maryland, The Guardian reported.
Police said witnesses seated in the back of the car told investigators that Webber, who was driving, opened fire and shot the front-seat passenger during the argument.
The Charles County Sheriff's Office in a statement said Webber later pulled over and asked the back-seat passengers to help remove the victim from the car.
However, they refused, got out, and left the scene. Webber then drove off with the victim still inside.
All occupants in the car were known to one another.
Police said the two witnesses later flagged down patrol officers to report the incident, prompting a search in areas where Webber was believed to have fled.
Nearly two hours later, officers responded to a report of a body found in a yard, where Wells was pronounced dead at the scene.
Detectives from the Charles County Sheriff's Office obtained a warrant for Webber's arrest and later found his car in Charlottesville, Virginia.
He was located at a nearby hospital where he had sought treatment for a medical issue.
Upon his discharge, Webber was arrested by officers from the Albemarle County Police Department and charged as a fugitive from justice.
"He is awaiting extradition to Charles County, where he will face charges including first- and second-degree murder, as well as other related offences," the statement said.
The Guardian reported that court documents allege Webber knew the passengers through work and had shot Wells twice in the head.
Webber first gained prominence in 2010 when ESPN featured him as a wrestler and football player despite not having hands or feet. He later became a professional cornhole player after losing his limbs to a severe bacterial infection as an infant.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MARKET PULSE AM MARCH 25, 2026 [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Bursa Malaysia opened firmer on Wednesday at 1,722, buoyed by improved risk appetite.
The benchmark index is expected to move within a narrow range of 1,700 to 1,720 today.
Sunway Healthcare emerged as the most actively traded counter at the opening bell, climbing to RM2.10, up five per cent, or 10 sen, from its previous close of RM2.
It saw a trading volume of about 28 million shares.
In the cryptocurrency market, Bitcoin is trading around RM279,609 and Ethereum climbed to RM8,541.
That's it for Market Pulse.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'Hero' Australian dog who saved 100 koalas retires
Article:
SYDNEY: An Australian dog credited with saving over 100 koalas from bushfires is retiring after a decade of service.
Bear, an 11-year-old Australian Koolie, was one of the first dogs in the country to be trained on the scent of koala fur.
The International Fund for Animal Welfare called using dogs to detect koalas a "novel" approach.
"No one knew if it could be done," IFAW head of programmes Josey Sharrad wrote in a statement about Bear on Monday.
As a pup, the four-legged hero's boundless energy made it tough to stay indoors, but he found his true potential in the bush.
"He literally went from chewing the walls of a Gold Coast apartment to roaming through the Aussie bush on a mission to save our most iconic species," Sharrad said.
Bear's skills saved over 100 koalas as the Black Summer bushfires raged across Australia's eastern seaboard from late 2019 to early 2020, razing millions of hectares, destroying thousands of homes and blanketing cities in noxious smoke.
The tail-wagging detective with a "joyful and goofy" personality retires with an extensive list of accolades -- including an Animal of the Year award and Puppy Tales Photos Australian Dog of the Year award.
He also features in a "dogumentary" called "Bear: Koala Hero", and in a book, "Bear to the Rescue".
Bear will embark on a slower-paced chapter on the Sunshine Coast with one of his former handlers, getting belly rubs and playing his favourite game, fetch.
One of his former handlers, Romane Cristescu, said Bear had been a "tireless ambassador for koalas for a decade".
"He melted hearts all around the world, and opened many doors so we could have critical and difficult conversations about climate change and its impacts on the threatened koalas, as well as so many other species." - AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Eversendai slides on project termination
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Eversendai Corp Bhd's share price fell to its lowest level since April 2024 after receiving a termination notice for a project in Saudi Arabia.
The stock opened at 32 sen, below its previous close of 35.5 sen, and continued to decline, dropping 22.54 per cent or 8 sen to 27.5 sen at the time of writing.
Trading activity was brisk, with 20.77 million shares changing hands, giving the company a market capitalisation of RM211.01 million.
Eversendai said the termination of its contract for the Neom Trojena Ski Village project was likely linked to the ongoing geopolitical tensions in the Middle East.
The company noted that its current order book stands at RM2.02 billion, excluding the remaining works under the Trojena Ski Village contract, while its tender book totals RM18.4 billion.
It added that it is close to finalising several new projects that are expected to boost its existing order book.
"Operations in the Middle East remain stable despite the current geopolitical situation. We remain highly optimistic in securing more projects across the Group to further improve our financial performance going forward," it said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Currency markets drift as traders sceptical of US efforts to end Iran war
Article:
SINGAPORE: Currency markets showed signs of fatigue early in Asian trade on Wednesday, with traders cautious over US President Donald Trump's efforts to bring an end to the war with Iran.
While Trump told reporters at the White House the US was making progress in talks with Iran, Tehran has denied that direct negotiations have taken place, keeping investors on edge.
The euro edged higher, up 0.1 per cent at US$1.1619, with most other currency pairs remaining unchanged. The British pound was up 0.1 per cent at US$1.3428, while the New Zealand dollar was flat at US$0.5834.
The subdued volatility contrasted with a surge in equity futures and a plunge in crude oil prices after Trump said on Tuesday the US was making progress in its efforts to negotiate an end to the war.
"For those reacting to every breaking headline around dialogue between the US and its allies and Iran, including speculation of high-level talks and temporary ceasefire proposals, an element of fatigue is now firmly setting in," said Chris Weston, head of research at Pepperstone Group Ltd in Melbourne.
Against the yen, the US dollar was steady at 158.645 yen, after the release of minutes from the Bank of Japan's January policy meeting showed many board members saw the need to keep raising interest rates without any specific pace in mind.
The Australian dollar fell as much as 0.2 per cent to US$0.6983 before rebounding to trade flat after the release of inflation data for February, which showed a 3.7 per cent rise prior to the start of the US-Israeli war with Iran, a slightly slower pace than expected by analysts.
Although markets still anticipate no change in US interest rates this year, expectations of policy tightening are rising. Fed funds futures now imply a 30.2 per cent chance of a 25-basis-point hike at the Federal Reserve's December meeting, up sharply from 8.2 per cent a day earlier, according to CME Group's FedWatch tool.
The Fed may need to keep interest rates steady "for some time" before further cuts are warranted, Fed Governor Michael Barr said on Tuesday, noting continued inflation above the Fed's two per cent target and the risks posed by the conflict in the Middle East.
Bond markets rebounded after a volatile week, with the yield on the US 10-year Treasury bond down 5 basis points at 4.338 per cent. "Higher oil prices added to expectations of increasing inflationary pressures and tighter monetary policy," analysts from Westpac wrote.
The US dollar index, which measures the greenback's strength against a basket of six currencies, was down 0.1 per cent at 99.126.
In cryptocurrencies, bitcoin climbed 1.2 per cent to US$70,910.16 while ether was up 0.8 per cent at US$2,164.74.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Press Metal gains on aluminium upswing
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Press Metal Aluminium Holdings Bhd is expected to benefit from elevated aluminium prices driven by ongoing geopolitical tensions, according to Hong Leong Investment Bank Bhd (HLIB Research).
HLIB Research raised the group's financial year 2026 and 2027 (FY26/FY27) earnings forecasts by 13 per cent and 18.7 per cent, respectively, driven by higher London Metal Exchange (LME) aluminium price assumptions, revised hedging positions and stronger Main Japanese Port (MJP) premiums.
The firm also upgraded the stock to "Buy" from "Hold", with a higher target price of RM8.63 from RM7.64.
HLIB Research said the escalation of the US-Iran conflict has pushed LME aluminium prices above US$3,400 per metric tonne, raising the likelihood of the global market shifting from a modest surplus in 2025 to a deficit in 2026 due to supply disruptions.
It noted that Press Metal stands to gain from the current environment, supported by higher aluminium prices, softer alumina costs and stronger demand from the Asia Pacific region as buyers seek alternative supply sources.
"A disruption to the Strait of Hormuz lasting more than one month could result in a deficit of up to 2.29 million tonnes, driven by logistical constraints that effectively strand Gulf exports, with smelter restart cycles estimated at three to six months.
"In a more severe scenario, where the Strait remains closed for an extended period of more than a month, the deficit could widen to 2.76 million tonnes, with restart timelines potentially stretching to six to 12 months," it said in a note.
HLIB Research said the region accounts for about nine per cent of global aluminium production but depends heavily on imported raw materials, leaving smelters vulnerable to disruptions in key shipping routes such as the Strait of Hormuz.
Several producers have already been affected, with Qatalum having earlier planned a shutdown before scaling back operations, while Aluminium Bahrain has begun curtailing capacity due to shipment disruptions.
HLIB Research added that Emirates Global Aluminium remains particularly exposed due to its reliance on imported raw materials, while smelters in Iran face elevated risks from ongoing military activity.
On the demand side, supply tightness is also emerging in Western markets, with the European Union and the United States heavily reliant on Middle Eastern aluminium imports, driving regional premiums higher.
Meanwhile, alumina prices are expected to remain subdued due to structural oversupply, which should support margins for producers like Press Metal.
"We expect the global alumina market to remain structurally oversupplied over the medium to long term, which should keep prices persistently soft, hovering around the US$300 a tonne," the firm said.
HLIB Research said the group has hedged about 65 per cent of its aluminium output at US$2,750 to US$2,800 per tonne, providing earnings visibility, while the remaining 35 per cent offers upside exposure to prevailing spot prices.
It said Press Metal is also seeing increased orders from the Asia-Pacific region, as customers look for immediate supply alternatives amid ongoing disruptions.
"We believe Press Metal stands to benefit from the current geopolitical backdrop, supported by elevated LME aluminium prices alongside lower alumina costs," it said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Water supply disruption dampens Hari Raya festivities for residents of Gua Musang
Article:
GUA MUSANG: Water cuts have hit Gua Musang, disrupting Hari Raya festivities as residents in Tanah Putih face prolonged clean water shortages.
Galas assemblyman Datuk Mohd Syahbuddin Hashim said he had received numerous complaints, adding the long-running problem worsened ahead of Hari Raya last weekend.
"I received many complaints about clean water supply problems, especially as Aidilfitri approached," he said.
He said homes in higher areas had faced disruptions for seven months, with conditions deteriorating during the festive period.
"The problem became more severe during Hari Raya as returning families struggled without water for bathing and cooking," he added.
Gua Musang is a largely rural district in southern Kelantan, near the border with Pahang.
It is characterised by small towns, villages and plantations, with limited urban development compared to major cities.
Mohd Syahbuddin said water tankers provided only short-term relief and did not resolve the clean water supply issue.
"Therefore, I urge authorities to identify the cause of the disruption in Galas and implement immediate and long-term measures to address the issue burdening residents," he said according to Harian Metro.
Meanwhile, a Tanah Putih resident, Nur Hidayah Ramli, 35, said she had to buy boxes of mineral water after supplies ran out ahead of Hari Raya, adding that it increased their Raya costs.
"We were very disappointed when water ran out and had to call a tanker to refill our tank. I hope the issue is resolved soon as it has persisted for a long time," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 7 foreigners denied entry at Bukit Kayu Hitam over invalid tickets, dubious claims
Article:
BUKIT KAYU HITAM: The Border Control and Protection Agency (MCBA) here yesterday denied entry to seven foreigners, including six Indian nationals, after they failed to provide a credible purpose of travel and were found with invalid return flight tickets.
Bukit Kayu Hitam MCBA commander Senior Assistant Commissioner Mohd Nasarudin M Nasir said the group of Indian nationals also tried to mislead Immigration officers by claiming they did not know one another, but checks revealed they held flight tickets with identical details.
"All were denied entry after failing to provide a credible purpose of travel and for possessing invalid return tickets," he said in a statement today.
A Cambodian woman was also denied entry after giving doubtful and inconsistent information to Immigration officers.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Kim vows North Korea's 'unshakable' support for Russia
Article:
SEOUL: North Korea's leader Kim Jong Un said his country will always support Russia, state media reported on Wednesday, in a thankyou letter to his Russian counterpart Vladimir Putin.
Ties between the two have grown closer since Moscow's 2022 invasion of Ukraine, with Pyongyang dispatching ground troops and weapons systems to aid Russia's war effort.
Analysts say the assistance has been provided in exchange for Russia's provision of food and weapons technologies.
"I express my sincere thanks to you for sending warm and sincere congratulations first on my reassumption of the heavy duty as President of the State Affairs," Kim said in the message on Tuesday, the official Korean Central News Agency said.
North Korea's rubber-stamp legislature reappointed Kim this week as head of the authoritarian nation's highest policymaking and governing body.
"Today the DPRK and Russia are closely cooperating to defend the sovereignty of the two countries," Kim said, using the initials of the North's official name.
"Pyongyang will always be with Moscow. This is our choice and unshakable will," he added.
South Korean and Western intelligence agencies have estimated that the North has sent thousands of soldiers to Russia, primarily to the Kursk region, along with artillery shells, missiles and long-range rocket systems.
Around 2,000 troops have been killed and thousands more have been wounded, according to South Korean estimates.
Putin and Kim struck a military deal during Putin's visit to Pyongyang in June 2024, obligating both states to provide military assistance "without delay" in the event of an attack on the other.
Kim's letter follows Belarusian state media reports that President Alexander Lukashenko would visit North Korea on a two-day trip from Wednesday "to strengthen bilateral cooperation."
Both countries have provided support for Russia in its war on Ukraine and are adversaries of the West.
The North has yet to confirm the visit. - AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Bursa Malaysia opens higher on improved regional risk appetite
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Bursa Malaysia opened higher on Wednesday, supported by improved risk appetite across regional markets despite a weaker overnight performance on Wall Street.
At 9.10am, the FTSE Bursa Malaysia KLCI (FBM KLCI) rose 16.61 points, or 0.97 per cent, to 1,725.37 from Tuesday's close of 1,708.76. The key index opened 13.74 points higher at 1,722.50.
The market breadth was positive, with 257 gainers leading 139 losers. A total of 238 counters were unchanged, 2,122 were untraded, and 24 were suspended.
Turnover stood at 168.76 million shares worth RM176.52 million.
Rakuten Trade Sdn Bhd vice president of equity research Thong Pak Leng noted that Wall Street closed lower amid selling in technology and healthcare stocks. The Dow Jones Index lost 0.18 per cent to end at 46,124.06.
He expects trading activity to become cautious, with investors reluctant to open new long positions amid lingering uncertainties.
Sector-wise, he said interest is expected to remain centred on oil and gas counters, while crude palm oil prices holding above RM4,500 per tonne are likely to provide trading opportunities within the plantation space.
"In the absence of strong catalysts, the benchmark index is expected to trade within a narrow range of 1,700 to 1,720 for today as investors continue to monitor geopolitical developments and commodity price trends," he said.
Among heavyweight counters, Maybank added 18 sen to RM11.52, Public Bank rose nine sen to RM4.95, CIMB and IHH Healthcare gained eight sen to RM7.91 and RM9.06, while Tenaga Nasional rose six sen to RM14.34.
On the most active list, Sunway Healthcare topped up 10 sen to RM2.10, AirAsia X was four sen higher at RM1.21, Eversendai fell nine sen to 26.5 sen, Borneo Oil was flat at half a sen, and Capital A increased half a sen to 41 sen.
Among the top gainers, Hong Leong Bank added 46 sen to RM22.92, Petronas Dagangan rose 38 sen to RM21.98, Fraser & Neave bounced 34 sen to RM30.06, and Nestle went up 28 sen to RM98.72.
Top losers included Petronas Chemicals, which slid 26 sen to RM5.54, while Batu Kawan went down 22 sen to RM19.58, PPB Group slid 12 sen to RM11.38, and MISC fell seven sen to RM8.21.
On the index board, the FBM Top 100 Index jumped 103.30 points to 12,386.97, the FBM Emas Index strengthened 97.29 points to 12,519.10, and the FBM ACE Index firmed 18.71 points to 4,338.64.
The FBM 70 Index surged 72.08 points to 16,984.34, and the FBM Emas Shariah Index recovered 65.10 points to 12,236.72.
By sector, the Financial Services Index climbed 263.15 points to 20,644.09, the Plantation Index rose 10.83 points to 8,649.30, and the Industrial Products and Services Index inched up 0.40 of a point to 181.05.
The Energy Index lost 4.79 points to 783.21.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Tomb raiders: From Hong Kong to Malaysia, urn thefts over the years
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Reports that more than 50 urns were stolen from three different columbaria within a month have sparked a federal police investigation, while families continue to hope for the return of their loved ones' remains.
In the case of the 22 urns stolen from Perpetual Memorial Park in Kulai, little is known about their whereabouts or what the thieves want with them.
This contrasts with the urns stolen from Xiao En Memorial Park and Nirvana, where the thieves demanded ransoms. The difference is that, in Nirvana's case, the urns were recovered.
This is not the first case of urn theft, nor is it the first time ransoms for the dead have been demanded from the living.
NST takes a look at prominent urn theft cases from around the world.
Legends' ashes returned
In Hong Kong, the ashes of renowned martial arts director Lau Kar-leung and veteran film producer Jimmy Heung were recovered in February, Dim Sum Daily reported.
Their urns were among several belonging to prominent figures and wealthy families that were reported missing from Po Fook Hill Cemetery.
Lau's widow, Mary Jean Reimer, said images of the missing urn were later sent to her in an attempt to extort ransom money, reportedly amounting to US$1 million.
Missing urn and an email address
In 2014, a family was told to pay 600,000 yuan for the return of an urn stolen from its family plot.
In place of the missing urn was an email address. Police later traced the suspect after he withdrew 10,000 yuan from a partial ransom payment.
The withdrawal at an ATM allowed authorities to obtain a clear image of his face, and he was later arrested.
Investigations revealed the suspect had targeted the site after reading claims that the family was the wealthiest in the area.
Money paid, then more demanded
Another case in China in 2015 involved a criminal syndicate targeting cemeteries in Henan province.
According to a Xinhua report, a victim, identified only as Liu, was informed that her husband's urn had been stolen.
The thieves left a mobile phone number, which Liu called to negotiate the return of the remains.
The gang initially demanded a ransom of 20,000 yuan. However, after Liu paid the initial amount, the gang demanded more money. It is not known whether the ashes were ever returned to Liu.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia may face hottest El Niño on record
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A climatology and climate change expert expects this year to record the world's highest temperatures, driven by the El Niño phenomenon and the effects of global warming.
Climatology and climate change expert Professor Emeritus Dr Fredolin Tangang said the El Niño phase has the potential to raise global temperatures, noting that 2024 was declared the hottest year on record following a 1.55 degrees celsius increase.
The Academy of Sciences Malaysia fellow said temperatures in 2025 dipped slightly compared with 2024 due to the influence of the La Niña phenomenon (the cool phase in the global climate cycle).
However, the continued impact of global warming meant that last year was still the third hottest on record.
The Climate Prediction Centre in the United States has forecast that El Niño will occur between July and August which will intensify towards the end of the year and into early 2027.
It is expected to weaken between March and April before ending in May next year.
"Based on forecasts, the probability of El Niño emerging during the southwest monsoon season exceeds 70 per cent, rising to around 80 to 90 per cent towards the end of this year," he told Harian Metro.
El Niño refers to a condition of extreme environmental warming, where sea surface temperatures rise above average.
This extreme temperature indicator refers to ambient temperatures exceeding 37 degrees celsius, which can rise to as high as 40 degrees celsius, accompanied by low humidity and dry conditions.
Elaborating further, Fredolin said signs of El Niño occur when the thermocline layer in the eastern Pacific Ocean weakens, reducing the upwelling of cooler water to the sea surface.
"This leads to rising sea temperatures, which in turn triggers the onset of El Niño.
"So far, these signs have just begun to appear on the sea surface in the eastern Pacific Ocean.
"Based on my more than three decades of research on El Niño, we are likely to face a strong El Niño event this year through to early next year," he said.
He added that the dry conditions will increase the risk of drought, forest fires and haze this year.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia’s private hospitals report no disruption to medicine supply despite West Asia war, says APHM
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, March 25 — Medicine supply in private hospitals across the country remains stable amid the war in West Asia, the Association of Private Hospitals Malaysia (APHM) said today.
The association said the country’s pharmaceutical supply chain has not reported any indications of being directly affected by the ongoing conflict.
However, the association said it will closely monitor the developments in the region and the subsequent impact on the cost of medicines and medical consumables.
“We will keep reviewing for any indications of a significant increase in the cost of medicines and medical consumables that may arise as a result of higher oil prices or global shipping disruptions from the conflict.
“APHM assures Malaysians that all essential healthcare services at private hospitals remain fully operational,” it said in a statement today.
The conflict between US-backed Israel and Iran that broke out on February 28 has driven up the cost of medicines due to a hike in the prices of raw materials and shipment delays triggered by blockades at the Strait of Hormuz.
Since the war began, Iran has effectively blocked the Strait of Hormuz, a major energy and trade chokepoint, but said on Sunday that it will allow passage for “non-hostile” vessels.
However, Malaysia’s Health Ministry said on Monday that the country’s medicine supply remains stable and assured that the short-term impact of the conflict is minimal.
The ministry said the medicine stocks in its facilities are maintained between one and three months based on usage requirements, along with an additional buffer stock of up to two months at the concession company level for medicines supplied through the logistics concession.

Title: On Police Day, IGP says PDRM’s 219-year legacy shows unwavering commitment to national security
Article:
=KUALA LUMPUR, March 25 — The Royal Malaysia Police (PDRM) remains a steadfast bulwark in preserving national security and stability, with more than two centuries of history and sacrifice.
Inspector-General of Police Datuk Seri Mohd Khalid Ismail said that behind the blue uniform worn by officers lie countless stories of courage, sacrifice and selflessness, given without expectation of reward.
He said that with resilience, expertise and the adoption of modern policing and technology, the police force continues to move forward in facing contemporary challenges to ensure the nation’s sovereignty is safeguarded.
“We, the Sang Saka Biru, will continue to stand firm, faithfully carrying out our duty, never fearful and never retreating in preserving the peace and harmony of the people,” he said in a post on the PDRM’s official Facebook page, extending wishes for the 219th Police Day.
Mohd Khalid also described the continued trust and support of the public as the lifeblood of the force’s strength in ensuring Malaysia remains peaceful, stable and prosperous.
In the same post, Deputy Inspector-General of Police Tan Sri Ayob Khan Mydin Pitchay, PERKEP Malaysia chairman Datin Seri Datuk Dr Munirah Che Rose, and vice-chairman Datuk Zalena Mohd Ariffin also conveyed their appreciation in conjunction with Police Day celebrations. — Bernama

Title: Seven-year-old boy dies after car hits him while crossing road in Kota Kuala Muda
Article:
SUNGAI PETANI, March 25 — A seven-year-old boy was killed after being hit by a car while crossing the road along Jalan Kota Kuala Muda near here last night.
Muhammad Ammar Zamri, a pupil of Sekolah Kebangsaan Kota Kuala Muda, was believed to have suddenly crossed the road before being struck by a Perodua Myvi.
Kuala Muda police chief ACP Hanyan Ramlan said the incident occurred at 9.23pm, with police receiving a report at 9.45pm.
“The car, driven by a man, was travelling from Kota Kuala Muda towards Pulau Sayak when the victim suddenly crossed in front of the moving vehicle.
“The close distance left the driver with no time to avoid the collision,” he said in a statement today.
The victim, who sustained injuries, was taken to the Kota Kuala Muda Health Clinic but was pronounced dead while receiving treatment.
The case is being investigated under Section 41 of the Road Transport Act, he added. — Bernama

Title: Saifuddin Nasution commends police for being ever-ready to safeguard the country
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, March 25 — The role of a police officer is more than just a profession; it is a solemn responsibility to the nation, said Minister of Home Affairs Datuk Seri Saifuddin Nasution Ismail.
He praised members of the Royal Malaysia Police (PDRM) for being ever-ready to perform their duties at any hour and under any circumstances, ensuring the country’s peace and security are upheld.
“Today, we celebrate an institution that has stood steadfast in safeguarding the nation for over two centuries — the PDRM.
“I extend my deepest appreciation and gratitude to all officers for your dedication and sacrifices, which are always valued and remembered,” he said in a Facebook post, extending Happy 219th Police Day greetings.
Saifuddin Nasution also expressed his hope that the PDRM will continue to uphold professionalism and integrity, remaining an institution that is close to the hearts of the people.
Communications Minister Datuk Fahmi Fadzil also extended his heartfelt greetings, expressing his appreciation for the police, particularly as they continue to carry out their duties.
“Thank you for your dedication and sacrifices in maintaining the nation’s peace and security. May you remain steadfast, resilient, and perform your duties with unwavering integrity, trust, and responsibility. Happy Police Day!” he wrote on Facebook.
Meanwhile, Minister in the Prime Minister’s Department (Federal Territories) Hannah Yeoh also conveyed her greetings in celebration of the 219th Police Day. — Bernama

Title: From ‘agak‑agak’ to ‘Malaysia boleh’: Oxford English Dictionary adds new Malaysia and Singapore words in latest update
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, March 25 — The Oxford English Dictionary (OED) has added several Malaysian and Singaporean English terms in its latest update, highlighting the region’s linguistic influence on global English.
Oxford Languages announced the additions in a press release yesterday, noting that many of the new entries originate from Malay and Hokkien.
The update explains that “
agak‑agak
” appears as both a
noun
and a
verb
, describing estimation or guesswork, and is “most frequently used in the context of a particular way of cooking, in which ingredients are added based on estimation and intuition rather than accurate measurement.”
Oxford Languages said Malaysians and Singaporeans commonly apply this intuitive style of cooking to dishes such as
assam laksa
and
ice kacang
.
The update also includes Malay borrowings such as “
wayang
,” which originally referred to traditional Indonesian and Malaysian theatrical performances involving puppetry or human dancers depicting mythological stories.
Oxford Languages notes that the word later developed a figurative noun sense meaning “a pretence or a false display intended to cheat or deceive,” as well as an adjective sense describing something “flashy, showy, or fake.”
“
Boleh
,” another Malay borrowing, appears as an intransitive verb meaning to have the power, ability, or capacity to do or deal with something successfully.
The update adds that it can also function like the modal verb “can,” and that its use was popularised by the slogan “Malaysia
boleh
” during a 1993 Milo marketing campaign supporting Malaysian athletes at the South-east Asian (SEA) Games in Singapore.
Hokkien contributions in this update include “
jialat
,” which describes a difficult or terrible situation. It originally also carried the sense of something irksome or exhausting, deriving from the Hokkien
chia̍h‑la̍t
, a compound of
chia̍h
meaning “to eat” and
la̍t
meaning “strength.”
Another Hokkien borrowing is “
kaypoh
,” which refers to a busybody and can function as a
noun
, adjective or
verb
.
The OED also adds “
BTO
,” the initialism for Singapore’s Build‑to‑Order public housing scheme, which has evolved into a
verb
meaning to apply for such a flat.
The press release states that applying for a BTO flat is often viewed as a relationship milestone, signalling that a couple is serious and “not there to
play play
(to fool about, mess around, or waste time).”
